content.title: H&M Checkout Redesign
content.summary: Reducing cart abandonment from 60% to 30% through a streamlined mobile-first checkout experience

content.caption: Before/after checkout: old 6-step cluttered checkout vs. new clean 3-step flow
content.alt: Hero comparison of old vs new H&M checkout

content.body:
H&M was losing 60% of shoppers at the one moment that mattered most — checkout.
Not because the products were wrong. Not because the prices were off. Because the experience of actually buying something was broken.
Six steps. Redundant form fields. No visible security signals. A mobile layout that ignorebs actually work. Shoppers were filling carts and leaving — and the data made it impossible to ignore.
This was the one problem worth solving.

content.caption: Only 24% of users completed checkout—losing over 75% of potential customers—because of friction at every step: forced account creation, confusing delivery options, and unclear payment confirmation

content.body:
I wanted to understand not just where people were dropping off — but why.

Starting with H&M Brain analytics, I mapped key drop-off points across the funnel. I then spoke with 20+ shoppers and ran a broader survey to add quantitative context to those patterns.

A few consistent themes emerged:

items: Friction from length. The six-step flow felt long and open-ended. Without a clear sense of progress, many users exited early.
Trust gaps. A significant portion of users expressed hesitation around payment security, yet there were no clear trust signals at that moment.
Mobile misalignment. Form fields, tap targets, and CTAs were designed with desktop in mind, but most users were completing the flow on mobile.

content.body: These patterns showed up across both interviews and survey responses, pointing to the same underlying issues from different angles.

content.caption: Research uncovered that nearly half of users struggle with trust at checkout, delivery clarity, and size confidence—revealing the top opportunities to reduce friction and increase conversions

content.body: I set targets that tied directly to business outcomes — not just design improvements.

items:
Collapsing 6 steps to 3
Bag review, delivery, and payment were restructured into a single continuous flow with inline editing. No more jumping backward. No more re-entering data.

Trust signals at the point of doubt
Security badges, encrypted payment indicators, and a clear returns policy were surfaced at the payment step — exactly where 40% of users were dropping off.

Mobile-first layout from the first wireframe
Thumb zones determined button placement. Form fields were full-width with large touch targets. Auto-fill and Apple/Google Pay were prioritised as primary payment paths, not afterthoughts.

Progress transparency
A minimal 3-step progress indicator gave users a finish line. Subtle micro-animations confirmed each action without demanding attention.

WCAG AA accessibility — built in, not bolted on
Colour contrast, focus states, and screen reader compatibility were part of the component spec from day one.

content.caption: Wireframes / Early Exploration low fidelity progression showing 3–4 iterations of the checkout flow.
Final high-fidelity mobile screens of the checkout flow

content.body:
Working alongside the DS lead, I extended H&M's existing design system to support the redesign at scale.
This inclding the token library (colour, spacing, typography, elevation), building reusable checkout-specific components — form fields, progress indicators, trust badges, payment selectors — and documenting interaction states for engineering handoff.
The output wasn't just a new checkout. It was infrastructure the broader team could build on.

The redesign shipped across web, iOS, and Android within the 4-week sprint.
Cart abandonment dropped from 60% to 30%.
Conversion rate increased 80% — from 2PS rose from 42 to 68.
The metrics confirmed what the research had already shown: the problem was never motivation. Shoppers wanted to buy. The experience just needed to get out of the way.

Guest checkout was de-prioritised due to sprint scope. In hindsight, forcing account creation likely accounts for a remaining portion of abandonment. That would be the next experiment — reducing the account creation barrier without compromising H&M's loyalty data strategy.
